P a g e  6 / 9  
 
Justification for Selection 
• Architectural Alignment: Our core depth model, Depth Anything (v2), was 
specifically pre-trained and fine-tuned using the NYU-Depth V2 distribution. 
By utilizing the same dataset for our internal validation, we ensure that the 
model’s weight biases and environmental assumptions (indoor lighting, 
typical ceiling heights, floor textures) match our target use case. 
• Standardized Benchmarking: NYU-Depth V2 provides high-resolution

ground truth labels in meters, allowing us to accurately measure absolute 
error—a critical metric for a navigation system where knowing if an obstacle 
is at 0.5m vs 1.5m is vital. 
 
3.2. Comparative Model Benchmarking 
To determine the best model for real-time inference on a portable device (Raspberry 
Pi 5), we evaluated five SOTA (State-of-the-Art) models. The evaluation focused on 
accuracy (AbsRel, RMSE, 𝑑1) versus operational speed (FPS).

Benchmarking Analysis 
• The Accuracy-Speed Trade-off: While ZoeDepth achieved the highest 
accuracy ($d1=0.955$), its inference speed of 0.3 FPS on a Raspberry Pi 5 
makes it unusable for live navigation. 
• Optimal Candidates: DA-V2-Small (Relative) and MiDaS v2.1 Small 
emerged as the most viable candidates. While MiDaS is faster (6.0 FPS), 
Depth Anything V2 (Small) provides a superior balance of spatial awareness
and stability, which is necessary for our risk-based deterministic logic.
